title: Sliding Window Maximum
description: Find maximum element in every window of size k

examples.explanation:
Window 0: indices 0-2 = [1,3,-1], max = 3
Window 1: indices 1-3 = [3,-1,-3], max = 3
Window 2: indices 2-4 = [-1,-3,5], max = 5
Window 3: indices 3-5 = [-3,5,3], max = 5
Window 4: indices 4-6 = [5,3,6], max = 6
Window 5: indices 5-7 = [3,6,7], max = 7
Result: [3,3,5,5,6,7]

Window 0: indices 0-3 = [8,5,10,7], max = 10
Window 1: indices 1-4 = [5,10,7,9], max = 10
Window 2: indices 2-5 = [10,7,9,4], max = 10
Window 3: indices 3-6 = [7,9,4,15], max = 15
Window 4: indices 4-7 = [9,4,15,12], max = 15
Window 5: indices 5-8 = [4,15,12,90], max = 90
Window 6: indices 6-9 = [15,12,90,13], max = 90
Result: [10,10,10,15,90,90,90]

approaches.name: Brute Force O(nk)
approaches.summary: For each window of size k, iterate through all k elements to find the maximum. This is straightforward but inefficient for large inputs.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Brute Force Sliding Window Maximum
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of finding maximum in every window of size k using brute force iteration.
ladders.explanation:
This is the complete brute force solution to the sliding window maximum problem. The intuition is straightforward: for each possible window position, we examine all k elements within that window to find the maximum value. While conceptually simple, this approach becomes inefficient for large inputs due to its O(nk) time complexity. The algorithm works by sliding a window of size k across the array from index 0 to n-k. For each window position, we initialize a maximum variable with the first element of the window, then iterate through the remaining k-1 elements, updating the maximum whenever we find a larger value. After processing all k elements, we record the maximum for that window and move to the next position. The space complexity is O(1) since we only use a few variables regardless of input size. This connects directly to the key subroutine (L1) which is the window maximum finding logic. Common mistakes include: 1) Off-by-one errors in window boundaries (should go from 0 to n-k inclusive), 2) Forgetting to reset max for each new window, 3) Incorrect handling of edge cases like k=1 or k=n. The real pattern involves nested loops where the outer loop controls window position and the inner loop finds the maximum within each window.

Algorithm:
1. Initialize empty result list
2. For i from 0 to n-k:
   a. Set max_val = arr[i]
   b. For j from i+1 to i+k-1:
      i. If arr[j] > max_val: max_val = arr[j]
   c. Append max_val to result
3. Return result

Time: O(nk) where n is array length
Space: O(1) excluding output storage

examples.trace:
Window 0: indices 0-2 = [1,3,-1], max = 3
Window 1: indices 1-3 = [3,-1,-3], max = 3
Window 2: indices 2-4 = [-1,-3,5], max = 5
Window 3: indices 3-5 = [-3,5,3], max = 5
Window 4: indices 4-6 = [5,3,6], max = 6
Window 5: indices 5-7 = [3,6,7], max = 7
Result: [3,3,5,5,6,7]

Window 0: indices 0-3 = [8,5,10,7], max = 10
Window 1: indices 1-4 = [5,10,7,9], max = 10
Window 2: indices 2-5 = [10,7,9,4], max = 10
Window 3: indices 3-6 = [7,9,4,15], max = 15
Window 4: indices 4-7 = [9,4,15,12], max = 15
Window 5: indices 5-8 = [4,15,12,90], max = 90
Window 6: indices 6-9 = [15,12,90,13], max = 90
Result: [10,10,10,15,90,90,90]

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Find Maximum in Fixed Window
ladders.desc: Finding the maximum element within a fixed subarray/window of size k.
ladders.explanation:
This is the core computational unit of the brute force approach: given a starting index and window size k, find the maximum value among the next k elements. The intuition is that we need to examine each element in the window exactly once to determine which is largest. This is essentially the inner loop of the complete solution. We start by assuming the first element is the maximum, then compare it with each subsequent element in the window, updating our maximum whenever we find a larger value. This subroutine connects to the full problem (L0) as the repeated application of this function for each window position gives us all window maximums. Common mistakes include: 1) Incorrect loop boundaries (should process k elements total), 2) Forgetting to initialize max with the first element, 3) Off-by-one errors when calculating the end index. The time complexity is O(k) since we examine each of the k elements once. Space complexity is O(1) as we only store the current maximum.

Algorithm:
1. Input: array arr, start index i, window size k
2. Set max_val = arr[i]
3. For j from i+1 to i+k-1:
   a. If arr[j] > max_val: max_val = arr[j]
4. Return max_val

Time: O(k)
Space: O(1)

examples.trace:
Window indices 1-3 = [2,8,1]
Initialize max = arr[1] = 2
Compare with arr[2] = 8: 8 > 2, update max = 8
Compare with arr[3] = 1: 1 ≤ 8, max remains 8
Return 8

Window indices 2-5 = [2,9,4,1]
Initialize max = arr[2] = 2
Compare with arr[3] = 9: 9 > 2, update max = 9
Compare with arr[4] = 4: 4 ≤ 9, max remains 9
Compare with arr[5] = 1: 1 ≤ 9, max remains 9
Return 9

ladders.title: Core Logic — Sliding Window Iteration Pattern
ladders.desc: The technique of moving a fixed-size window across an array by incrementing start position.
ladders.explanation:
This level focuses on the sliding window iteration pattern itself, which is the fundamental technique behind the brute force approach. The intuition is that we can examine all contiguous subarrays of size k by starting at index 0 and moving the window one position at a time until we reach the last possible starting position (n-k). This creates a sequence of overlapping windows that cover all required subarrays. The key insight is that there are exactly n-k+1 windows of size k in an array of length n. This logic connects to L1 (finding maximum in each window) by providing the framework in which we apply the maximum-finding subroutine. Common mistakes include: 1) Incorrect calculation of number of windows (should be n-k+1, not n-k), 2) Going beyond valid array bounds, 3) Confusion between 0-based and 1-based indexing. The time complexity for just the iteration is O(n) since we have n-k+1 windows, but when combined with maximum finding it becomes O(nk). Space complexity is O(1).

Algorithm:
1. Input: array arr of length n, window size k
2. For i from 0 to n-k:
   a. Process window starting at index i
   b. (In full solution, find maximum here)

Time: O(n) for iteration alone
Space: O(1)

examples.trace:
n = 5, k = 3, n-k = 2
Window 0: start at i=0, indices 0-2 = [1,2,3]
Window 1: start at i=1, indices 1-3 = [2,3,4]
Window 2: start at i=2, indices 2-4 = [3,4,5]
Total windows: 3 = 5-3+1

n = 6, k = 4, n-k = 2
Window 0: start at i=0, indices 0-3 = [a,b,c,d]
Window 1: start at i=1, indices 1-4 = [b,c,d,e]
Window 2: start at i=2, indices 2-5 = [c,d,e,f]
Total windows: 3 = 6-4+1

ladders.title: Building Block — Finding Maximum in Array Segment
ladders.desc: Finding the maximum value in any contiguous segment of an array.
ladders.explanation:
This intermediate concept involves finding the maximum element within a specified range of array indices. The intuition is that we need to examine each element in the given range and keep track of the largest value encountered. This is a generalization of the window maximum problem without the sliding aspect. We start by initializing our maximum with the first element of the segment, then iterate through the remaining elements, updating the maximum whenever we find a larger value. This connects to L2 (sliding window iteration) because once we can find the maximum in any segment, applying this to consecutive segments gives us the sliding window maximum solution. Common mistakes include: 1) Incorrect initialization (should use first element of segment, not global first), 2) Off-by-one errors in loop boundaries, 3) Forgetting to handle empty segments. The time complexity is O(m) where m is the segment length. Space complexity is O(1).

Algorithm:
1. Input: array arr, start index s, end index e (inclusive)
2. Set max_val = arr[s]
3. For i from s+1 to e:
   a. If arr[i] > max_val: max_val = arr[i]
4. Return max_val

Time: O(e-s+1)
Space: O(1)

examples.trace:
Segment indices 1-4 = [2,9,1,7]
Initialize max = arr[1] = 2
Compare with arr[2] = 9: 9 > 2, update max = 9
Compare with arr[3] = 1: 1 ≤ 9, max remains 9
Compare with arr[4] = 7: 7 ≤ 9, max remains 9
Return 9

Segment indices 2-5 = [2,6,1,9]
Initialize max = arr[2] = 2
Compare with arr[3] = 6: 6 > 2, update max = 6
Compare with arr[4] = 1: 1 ≤ 6, max remains 6
Compare with arr[5] = 9: 9 > 6, update max = 9
Return 9

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Element Comparison and Maximum Update
ladders.desc: The fundamental operation of comparing two values and updating a maximum variable.
ladders.explanation:
This is the most basic computational unit in the brute force approach: comparing two numbers and updating a running maximum when a larger value is found. The intuition is simple - we maintain a variable that stores the largest value seen so far, and whenever we encounter a new element, we compare it with our current maximum. If the new element is larger, it becomes the new maximum; otherwise, we keep the existing maximum. This operation connects to L3 (finding maximum in segment) as repeated application of this comparison across all elements in a segment gives us the segment maximum. Common mistakes include: 1) Forgetting to initialize max before first comparison, 2) Using wrong comparison operator (should be > not ≥ if we want first occurrence), 3) Not actually updating the max variable when needed. The time complexity for a single comparison is O(1). Space complexity is O(1). This is essentially the core of any maximum-finding algorithm.

Algorithm:
1. Initialize max_val with first element
2. For each subsequent element x:
   a. If x > max_val: max_val = x
3. After all elements: max_val contains maximum

Time: O(1) per comparison
Space: O(1)

examples.trace:
Initialize max = 3
Compare 7 > 3: yes, update max = 7
Compare 2 > 7: no, max remains 7
Compare 9 > 7: yes, update max = 9
Compare 5 > 9: no, max remains 9
Final max = 9

Initialize max = 10
Compare 5 > 10: no, max remains 10
Compare 10 > 10: no (equal), max remains 10
Compare 3 > 10: no, max remains 10
Compare 8 > 10: no, max remains 10
Final max = 10

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Array Traversal with Indexing
ladders.desc: Basic array access and iteration using indices.
ladders.explanation:
This is the foundational concept needed for the brute force approach: understanding how to access array elements using indices and iterate through arrays. The intuition is that arrays store elements in contiguous memory locations, and we can access any element using its index (position). For traversal, we typically use a loop that increments an index variable to visit each element sequentially. This connects to L4 (element comparison) because once we can access array elements, we can compare them to find maximums. Common beginner mistakes include: 1) Off-by-one errors (arrays usually start at index 0), 2) Accessing indices beyond array bounds, 3) Confusion between index and value. The time complexity for traversing n elements is O(n). Space complexity is O(1) for the iteration itself.

Algorithm:
1. Initialize index i = 0
2. While i < array length:
   a. Access element at arr[i]
   b. Process element
   c. Increment i = i + 1

Time: O(n) for full traversal
Space: O(1)

examples.trace:
i=0: access arr[0]=10
i=1: access arr[1]=20
i=2: access arr[2]=30
i=3: access arr[3]=40
i=4: stop (4 ≥ array length 4)

i=0: access arr[0]=5
i=1: access arr[1]=3
i=2: access arr[2]=7
i=3: stop (3 ≥ array length 3)

approaches.name: Deque O(n)
approaches.summary: Use a double-ended queue to store indices of elements in decreasing order, ensuring the front always has the maximum for the current window. Efficiently removes elements outside the window.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Sliding Window Maximum with Deque
ladders.desc: Complete solution to find maximum in every window of size k using deque
ladders.explanation: The sliding window maximum problem requires finding the maximum element in every contiguous window of size k as it moves through an array. The brute force approach would be O(n*k) time, but we can achieve O(n) using a deque (double-ended queue). The key insight is to maintain a deque that stores indices of array elements in decreasing order of their values. This ensures the front of the deque always contains the index of the maximum element for the current window. As we slide the window, we: 1) Remove indices from the front that are outside the current window. 2) Remove indices from the back while the corresponding elements are smaller than the current element (maintaining decreasing order). 3) Add the current index to the back. 4) Once we've processed at least k elements, the front of the deque gives us the maximum for that window. The time complexity is O(n) because each element is added and removed from the deque at most once. Space complexity is O(k) for storing indices in the deque. This approach is optimal because we avoid repeatedly scanning the window. Common mistakes include: forgetting to remove out-of-window indices before checking the front, not handling the initial window correctly, and confusing indices with values. The connection to L1 is that this full solution integrates the deque maintenance subroutine with window tracking.

examples.trace:
Initialize deque = [], result = [].
Index 0 (value 1): deque = [0]. No output (window incomplete).
Index 1 (value 3): Remove indices from back while values < 3: remove index 0 (value 1 < 3). deque = [1]. No output.
Index 2 (value -1): Add to back: deque = [1,2]. Window complete (indices 0-2). Remove out-of-window indices from front: none (front index 1 is in window). Front index 1 has value 3 → result = [3].
Index 3 (value -3): Remove from back while values < -3: none (-1 > -3). deque = [1,2,3]. Remove out-of-window from front: index 1 is in window (1 ≥ 3-3+1=1). Front index 1 value 3 → result = [3,3].
Index 4 (value 5): Remove from back while values < 5: remove indices 3 (-3), 2 (-1), 1 (3). deque = [4]. Remove out-of-window: none. Front index 4 value 5 → result = [3,3,5].
Index 5 (value 3): Remove from back while values < 3: none (5 > 3). deque = [4,5]. Remove out-of-window: index 4 is in window (4 ≥ 5-3+1=3). Front index 4 value 5 → result = [3,3,5,5].
Index 6 (value 6): Remove from back while values < 6: remove indices 5 (3), 4 (5). deque = [6]. Remove out-of-window: none. Front index 6 value 6 → result = [3,3,5,5,6].
Index 7 (value 7): Remove from back while values < 7: remove index 6 (6). deque = [7]. Remove out-of-window: none. Front index 7 value 7 → result = [3,3,5,5,6,7].

Initialize deque = [], result = [].
Index 0 (9): deque = [0].
Index 1 (8): Remove from back while < 8: none (9 > 8). deque = [0,1].
Index 2 (7): Remove from back while < 7: none (8 > 7). deque = [0,1,2].
Index 3 (6): Remove from back while < 6: none (7 > 6). deque = [0,1,2,3]. Window complete. Remove out-of-window from front: none (0 ≥ 3-4+1=0). Front index 0 value 9 → result = [9].
Index 4 (5): Remove from back while < 5: none (6 > 5). deque = [0,1,2,3,4]. Remove out-of-window: front index 0 is out (0 < 4-4+1=1), remove it. deque = [1,2,3,4]. Front index 1 value 8 → result = [9,8].
Index 5 (4): Remove from back while < 4: none (5 > 4). deque = [1,2,3,4,5]. Remove out-of-window: front index 1 is out (1 < 5-4+1=2), remove it. deque = [2,3,4,5]. Front index 2 value 7 → result = [9,8,7].
Index 6 (3): Remove from back while < 3: none (4 > 3). deque = [2,3,4,5,6]. Remove out-of-window: front index 2 is out (2 < 6-4+1=3), remove it. deque = [3,4,5,6]. Front index 3 value 6 → result = [9,8,7,6].
Index 7 (2): Remove from back while < 2: none (3 > 2). deque = [3,4,5,6,7]. Remove out-of-window: front index 3 is out (3 < 7-4+1=4), remove it. deque = [4,5,6,7]. Front index 4 value 5 → result = [9,8,7,6,5].
Index 8 (1): Remove from back while < 1: none (2 > 1). deque = [4,5,6,7,8]. Remove out-of-window: front index 4 is out (4 < 8-4+1=5), remove it. deque = [5,6,7,8]. Front index 5 value 4 → result = [9,8,7,6,5,4,3].

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Maintaining Decreasing Order Deque
ladders.desc: Maintain a deque of indices where corresponding values are in decreasing order
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the heart of the sliding window maximum algorithm. Given an array and a current index, we maintain a deque that stores indices such that their corresponding array values are in strictly decreasing order from front to back. The intuition is that by keeping only potentially maximum candidates, we can quickly access the maximum at the front. The algorithm: 1) While the deque is not empty and the value at the current index is greater than the value at the back index, remove indices from the back. This ensures new larger elements remove smaller ones that can never be the maximum in future windows. 2) Add the current index to the back. This maintains the decreasing order property. Time complexity is amortized O(1) per operation because each index is added once and removed at most once. Space complexity is O(k) for the deque. The connection to L0 is that this subroutine, combined with window boundary checks, solves the full problem. Common mistakes include: not using indices (using values directly loses window position information), not handling equal values correctly (strict decreasing vs non-increasing), and removing from the wrong end. Real code pattern: while deque and nums[deque[-1]] < nums[i]: deque.pop().

examples.trace:
Start with deque = [].
Index 0 (value 5): deque empty → add 0 → deque = [0].
Index 1 (value 3): Back index 0 has value 5 > 3 → no removal. Add 1 → deque = [0,1].
Index 2 (value 4): Back index 1 has value 3 < 4 → remove back (index 1). Now back index 0 has value 5 > 4 → no removal. Add 2 → deque = [0,2].
Index 3 (value 2): Back index 2 has value 4 > 2 → no removal. Add 3 → deque = [0,2,3].

Start deque = [].
Index 0 (1): add 0 → [0].
Index 1 (2): Back 0 has 1 < 2 → remove 0. Add 1 → [1].
Index 2 (3): Back 1 has 2 < 3 → remove 1. Add 2 → [2].
Index 3 (2): Back 2 has 3 > 2 → no removal. Add 3 → [2,3].
Index 4 (1): Back 3 has 2 > 1 → no removal. Add 4 → [2,3,4].

ladders.title: Core Logic — Why Decreasing Order Gives Maximum
ladders.desc: Understanding why maintaining decreasing order in deque ensures front has maximum
ladders.explanation: The core logic is that by storing indices in decreasing order of their values, the front of the deque always contains the index of the maximum element relevant to the current window. Here's why this works: 1) Decreasing order means nums[deque[0]] ≥ nums[deque[1]] ≥ ... ≥ nums[deque[-1]]. So the front is the largest. 2) When we add a new element, we remove all smaller elements from the back because if a new element is larger, those smaller elements can never be the maximum in any future window that includes this new element (since the new element will always be larger and in the same window). 3) Elements are added in sequence, so the deque naturally maintains candidates that could be maximums. The intuition is similar to keeping a 'promising' list where we discard elements that are dominated by newer, larger ones. This is efficient because each element is processed once. The connection to L1 is that this logic justifies the subroutine of removing smaller elements from the back. Common mistakes include: thinking the deque stores values directly (losing index information for window bounds), not understanding why we remove from the back (not the front), and confusion about equal values (if we keep equal values, the front still has a maximum). Real code pattern: The decreasing order is enforced by the comparison nums[deque[-1]] < nums[i].

examples.output: Deque shows maximum at front through steps
examples.trace:
At index 0 (3): deque = [0] → front value 3 is max so far.
Index 1 (1): 1 < 3, so we keep both → deque = [0,1]. Front still 3 (max).
Index 2 (2): Compare with back: 1 < 2, so remove index 1. Now back is index 0 with value 3 > 2, so keep. deque = [0,2]. Front value 3 is still max. If we had window size 3, front gives max 3.

examples.output: New larger element removes older smaller ones
examples.trace:
Index 0 (1): deque = [0], front max = 1.
Index 1 (3): Compare with back: 1 < 3, so remove index 0. deque = [1], front max = 3. This shows 3 removes 1 because 3 is larger and will dominate in windows.
Index 2 (2): Compare with back: 3 > 2, so keep both. deque = [1,2], front max = 3. Even though 2 is added, 3 remains front as it's larger.

testCases.input: nums = [5], what is front value after processing?
nums = [4,2], what is front after index 1?
nums = [2,4], what is front after index 1?
nums = [3,3,3], what is front after each step?
nums = [6,5,7], what is front after index 2?
nums = [1,2,1], what is front after index 2?
nums = [9,8,10], what is front after index 2?
nums = [0,5,3], what is front after index 2?
nums = [7,1,6], what is front after index 2?
nums = [4,0,2], what is front after index 2?

ladders.title: Building Block — Sliding Window Boundary Management
ladders.desc: Track which indices are within the current window of size k
ladders.explanation: This building block handles the sliding aspect of the problem. Given a window size k and a current index i, we need to know which indices are within the window [i-k+1, i]. The key is to remove indices from the front of the deque that are outside this range. The intuition: as the window slides right, the left boundary moves, so indices less than i-k+1 are no longer in the window and should be discarded. Since we add indices in order, the deque front contains the oldest index, so we check if deque[0] < i-k+1. If true, we remove it from the front. This ensures the deque only contains indices relevant to the current window. Time complexity is O(1) per check. Space complexity is O(1) additional. The connection to L2 is that this boundary check, combined with the decreasing order logic, ensures the front has the maximum for the exact current window. Common mistakes include: removing from the wrong end (should be front), incorrect boundary calculation (use i-k+1 not i-k), and not checking before accessing the front (ensure deque not empty). Real code pattern: if deque and deque[0] < i - k + 1: deque.popleft().

examples.input: k=3, process indices with deque = [0,2,3] at i=4
examples.trace: Current index i=4, window is [4-3+1=2, 4]. Check front of deque: deque[0]=0. Is 0 < 2? Yes, so remove index 0 from front. New deque = [2,3]. Indices 2 and 3 are in window [2,4].

examples.input: k=2, process indices with deque = [1,2] at i=3
examples.trace: i=3, window [3-2+1=2, 3]. Front deque[0]=1. Is 1 < 2? Yes, remove index 1. New deque = [2]. Index 2 is in window [2,3].

testCases.input: k=2, i=2, deque=[0,1], should remove front?
k=4, i=5, deque=[2,3,4], should remove front?

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Deque Push and Pop from Both Ends
ladders.desc: Perform append, pop from right, and popleft from left on a deque
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves using a double-ended queue (deque) that allows efficient insertion and deletion from both ends. In Python, collections.deque provides O(1) time for append (add to right), pop (remove from right), and popleft (remove from left). The intuition: we need to add new indices to the right and remove from left (for window boundaries) or right (for maintaining order). This efficiency is crucial for the O(n) algorithm. Step-by-step: 1) Initialize an empty deque. 2) To add an index i: deque.append(i). 3) To remove from the right while condition holds: while deque and condition: deque.pop(). 4) To remove from the left if out of window: if deque and deque[0] < i-k+1: deque.popleft(). Time complexity is O(1) per operation amortized. Space complexity is O(k) for the deque. The connection to L3 is that these operations implement the boundary management. Common mistakes include: using list instead of deque (pop(0) is O(n)), confusing left and right ends, and not checking if deque is empty before accessing. Real code pattern: from collections import deque; d = deque(); d.append(1); d.pop(); d.popleft().

examples.input: Perform operations: append 0, append 1, pop, popleft on empty deque
examples.trace:
Start deque = [].
Append 0: deque = [0].
Append 1: deque = [0,1].
Pop (remove from right): removes 1 → deque = [0].
Popleft (remove from left): removes 0 → deque = [].

Initial deque = [2,3].
Append 4: deque = [2,3,4].
Popleft: remove 2 → deque = [3,4].
Append 5: deque = [3,4,5].

testCases.expected: After popleft: [7], after append: [7,8], after pop: [7]

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — What is a Sliding Window?
ladders.desc: Understand the idea of a fixed-size window moving through an array
ladders.explanation: A sliding window is a conceptual range of elements in an array that moves sequentially. For a window of size k, we start at the first k elements (indices 0 to k-1), then slide one position to the right to cover indices 1 to k, then 2 to k+1, and so on until the end. The intuition is to process contiguous subsets efficiently without recomputing from scratch. For example, with array [a,b,c,d] and k=2, windows are: [a,b], [b,c], [c,d]. This is foundational because many problems involve analyzing local patterns. Step-by-step: 1) Define window start and end indices. 2) Move end index one step right. 3) Adjust start index if window size exceeds k. Time complexity for sliding through n elements is O(n). Space complexity is O(1) for indices. The connection to L4 is that sliding window boundaries dictate when to remove elements from the deque. Common mistakes include: off-by-one errors in indices, not handling cases where k > array length, and misunderstanding window movement. Real code pattern: for i in range(len(nums)): # i is end index; start = i - k + 1.

examples.trace:
Start with window at indices 0-2: elements [1,2,3].
Slide right: indices 1-3: [2,3,4].
Slide right: indices 2-4: [3,4,5].
No more slides as window would go beyond array.

Window size 1: first at index 0: [10].
Slide to index 1: [20].
